name: attrs
what_it_does: Python library for writing boilerplate-free classes using @define and @attrs decorators. Automatically generates __init__, __repr__, __eq__, and other dunder methods. Predates and inspired Python's dataclasses. Offers validators, converters, slots support, and frozen (immutable) classes. attrs is the lower-level API; cattrs builds on it for serialization.
use_cases: Define Python data model classes with automatic __init__, __repr__, __eq__ generation without boilerplate using @define decorator
Add field-level validation with @validator and type converters to ensure data integrity at instantiation time
Create immutable value objects (frozen=True) for use as dict keys or in sets without implementing __hash__ manually
Use __slots__ automatically for memory-efficient classes in data-intensive applications (attrs @define uses slots by default)
Build structured configuration objects with defaults, validators, and type coercion for application configuration handling
not_for: ORM-style database models — use SQLAlchemy or Django ORM for models that map to database tables
Pydantic-style JSON serialization with rich validation — use Pydantic for API input validation and JSON schema generation
Cases where Python stdlib dataclasses are sufficient — @dataclass handles simple cases without adding a dependency
best_when: Building Python classes that need more power than dataclasses (validators, converters, slots) but less complexity than Pydantic.
avoid_when: You need JSON schema generation, FastAPI integration, or rich type coercion from external inputs — use Pydantic.

auth.notes: Pure Python library — no auth needed.

agent_readiness.error_message_notes: Validation failures raise attrs.exceptions.NotAnInstanceOf, attrs.exceptions.NotAnInstantiableType, or custom validator exceptions. Error messages include field names and expected types.
agent_readiness.idempotency_notes: Pure transformation library — class definitions are idempotent.
known_agent_gotchas: attrs has two APIs: the older @attr.s/@attrib API and the newer @define/@field API — don't mix them; prefer @define for new code
@define uses __slots__ by default which prevents adding arbitrary attributes at runtime — use @attrs(slots=False) if dynamic attributes are needed
Validators run at __init__ time only — mutable fields can be changed to invalid values after construction unless the class is frozen
Frozen classes (frozen=True) create a new copy on field changes via evolve() — not in-place mutation; agents must reassign the variable
Factory defaults use attr.Factory(list) not default=[] — using mutable defaults directly raises an exception (unlike dataclasses which silently share)
attrs validators use a separate @field.validator decorator pattern vs Pydantic's @validator — not interchangeable when migrating between libraries

security_score_components.security_notes: Pure Python library with no network access. No security concerns beyond application-level input validation in custom validators.
